inputs.label: Annual Demand (units)
inputs.businessContext: Total quantity of product demanded per year. Based on APICS/SCOR standards for demand planning.

inputs.label: Ordering Cost per Order ($)
inputs.businessContext: Fixed cost incurred per order (e.g., administrative, shipping). Referenced in EOQ model by Harris (1913) and widely used in inventory management.

inputs.label: Holding Cost per Unit per Year ($)
inputs.businessContext: Cost to hold one unit of inventory for one year, including storage, insurance, obsolescence. Based on standard inventory carrying cost calculations (APICS).

inputs.label: Unit Cost ($)
inputs.businessContext: Purchase cost per unit. Used to compute total inventory value and carrying cost as percentage of unit cost.

inputs.label: Carrying Cost Rate (% of unit cost per year)
inputs.businessContext: Annual carrying cost as a percentage of unit cost. Typical range 15-30% per year (industry benchmarks from WERC, APICS).

inputs.label: Lead Time (days)
inputs.businessContext: Time from order placement to receipt. Used for reorder point calculation. Standard in inventory management.

inputs.label: Safety Stock (units)
inputs.businessContext: Extra inventory held to mitigate uncertainty in demand or lead time. Based on service level and demand variability (Six Sigma).

inputs.label: Working Days per Year
inputs.businessContext: Number of operating days per year. Used to convert lead time to annual demand rate. Standard assumption.

thresholds.carryingCostRate > 30: Carrying cost rate exceeds typical industry benchmark (30%). Consider reducing inventory levels.
thresholds.safetyStock > 0.5 * annualDemand: Safety stock is more than 50% of annual demand. Review demand variability and lead time reliability.

suggestedActions: If carrying cost rate is high, consider reducing safety stock or negotiating lower storage costs.
If inventory turnover is low, review demand forecasting and consider JIT principles.
If EOQ is very large, evaluate order quantity discounts or split orders.
